CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Some of the imitation Suffragettes tried on some of
their tricks-at the Churchill meeting in Belfast, but they
appear to bave been very quickly ejected from the
marquee. What had Churchill at the Belfast meeting
to do with the English agitation for votes for women ?
Anyone with any sense of humour or senge of propor-
tion would see the utter absurdity of any of the gereech-
ing sisterhood interfering at the Belfast meeting. What-
ever sympathy there may have been for the imitation
Suffragettes in Ireland, we think most of it has evapo-
rated.  The performances at the Churchill meeting
must tend to get up some feeling against this imitation
party and its few male hangers-on and oddities. The
question of women’s franchise, 80 far ag Ireland is con-
cerned, is rightly one that should be held over for solu-
tion by an Irish Parliament.

We still are of opinion that the Government should
have promptly thrown the disloyal ruffians—Carson,
TLondonderry, and the rest—into jail, and that Churchill
should have held, his meeting in the Ulster Hall. How-
ever, as things turned out the Unionists have every
reason to growl at the stupid tactics of Londonderry
and Co.

The net result in the way of disturbance ab Belfast
aftor all was that a gang of Unionist ruffians made an
attack on—an hospitall  Shortly after ten o’clock ab
night a mob of Unionist miscreants attacked the Mater
Infirmorum Hospital. A regular fusilade of rivets and
large stones were thrown at the hospital. All honour
to the brave! How intrepid and fearless these Belfast
Unionist hearts were as they fired rivets, not knowing
but that at any moment a dying man, or a sick woman,
might get up and fling, say, a square of soap at them.
And then there was the gallant attack of 100 girl Union-
ists on two Catholic fellow-workers in & mill.

Having noted that Mr. John Redmond endorsed the
speech of Mr. Churchill, the ** Irish Times '’ remarks:
““We are confident that thinking Nationalists will re-
ceive this official endorsement with dismay. We have
maintained from the first—and every intelligent
Nationalist agrees with us—that, 1f Home Rule is in-
evitable, it must be complete Home Rule—Colonial
Home Rule—or nothing.”’ The italics are ours, and they
indicate some charming unconscious humour on the
part of the wobbling old “‘Irish Times.”” The * think-
ing’’ and the * intelligent "’ Nationalist, into whose
minds the “ Irish Times '’ has such insight, are much
akin to the ‘* educated Roman Catholic with whom
the ** Irish Times ’’ is also familiar. ~The trick of the
“Trish Times,”’ if it sees any chance, will be to try to
injure Home Rule by belittling the Bill. If it suits
its purpose, the ‘‘ Irish Times’’ will become quite an
extremist when the Bill is disclosed, and little short of
an Irish Republic will satisfy its patriotic heart. More
than the ** Irish Times > may attempt this game, and
the Irish Nationalist public will do well to watch the
* Independent > with the profoundest suspicion. For
whom does the * we '’ of the ‘ Independent ** stand?
Not surely the salaried cypher named Harrington, who
is the alleged editor; and if not he, who?

Any sort of anonymous libel on Catholic citizens is
evidently good enough for the ‘* Irish Times’’ these
days—though we suppose it has its eye open for libels
since the Canon McInerney blunder that cost it so
dearly. ~ In its issue of February 9th it inserted an
anonymous letter purporting to come from Dundalk.
Here is one paragraph from this anonymous—and, so
far as law and the ““Irish Times "’ go, safe—libel :—

«Tn Dundalk probably one-sixth of the popula-
$ion is non-Catholic and anti-Home Rule.  They
are largely of the trading and business community,
and they remain muzzled because the disasters
that would overwhelm them under Home Rule
would commence to take immediate effect upon
them were they to venture upon even an expression
of opinion, much less an active campaign. It would
b}a ;l caso of putting the shutters up sooner instead
of later.”

So according to this, Unionist traders in Dundalk are
afraid to express an opinion against Home Rule. That,
of ‘course, is o palpable lie, yet this ** Trish Times’’ pub-
lighed this lie from an anonymous correspondent. Bub

6
the tames and the ‘tolerance provers’ have them-
selves largely to blame for this lying. This is all they
- get for their tolerance proving:—

“ An unenviable position ours! If we come into
the open now we will be frozen out by the means
which the Nationalist knows too well how to use—
commercial ostracism.’’

Non-Catholic traders are prosperous in every part of
Catholic Ireland; the Catholics, or many of them, are
fond of going out ostentatiously to * prove their toler-
ance,”’ and that is the sort of thanks they get for it
from a false and cowardly anonymous correspondent in
the *‘ Irish Times.”” Here is another bit:—

* To-day every small Irish town has its anxious
loyalists, whose very existence hangs upon the
success of Ulster and the view the British electo-
rate takes of the situation. Our fingers itch to be
in the fray; our existence depends upon our keep-
ing out of it.”

We were not displeased to read on Saturday night
that the ‘‘Irish " teams got badly beaten at' Inter-
national Rugby and Association.  Association is a
great Tommy Atkins’ game, but, though it is played
fairly extensively in Ireland, an Irish team never beat
England in an International match yet. The Rugby
team got a bad beating too. Notwithstanding the
patronage of the Christian Brothers in Cork, of Black-
rock College, St. Mary’s College, and other places,
Ireland made a poor show on Saturday. Of course,
it is understood that there is a certain amount of anti-
Catholic bigotry at play when the selection of the Irish
team is being made. If that be so, it only goes to
show that, even when Blackrock College, the Christian
Brothers of Cork, St. Mary’s College, Rathmines, and
others send in their youths for Rugby, the lads appear
to be so tame and terrified that the ‘“ saved '’ are able
to rig the teams.

The ninth annual report of the King’s County Com-
mittee for Agriculture and Live Stock and of the
King’s County Joint Committee of Technical Instruc-
tion is an interesting document—indeed, as it runs to
54 pages, we may call it a volume. Demonstration
plots for the growing of fruit, vegetables, and flowers
were established in six different centres, and a small
farmer, Mr. James Buckley, of Ballycowan, who con-
ducted one of these plots under the supervision of the
Instructor, gives a very interesting and encouraging
account of the working.  Such reports as these are
part of the reading in which a masculine and progres-
sive Ireland would be interested.  They form part,
and an important part, of the story of modern Ireland
in the making.

If Home Rule comes off the Post Office in Ireland
will, of course, be administered under an Irish Parlia-
ment. At the present time what appears to be a
Unionist plot for which a Liberal Government is respon-
sible is being carried on. Changes in recent times are
calculated to place extra difficulties in the way when
the Post Office in Ireland comes to be placed under a
Home Rule Government. Up to some years ago the
Post Office Secretary in Ireland, Mr. Bgerton, with
whom we had a pleasant controversy about a year ago,
had control of the Dublin Post Office Stores Depot. It
was taken out of his control and placed under that of
some official in London: we don’t suppose poor old
Egerton made any fight. That was only one move in
the game. A few months ago the Dublin Stores Depot
was placed under the control of a minor official in Bir-
mingham, and Birmingham in its turn is under Lon-
don control. It is simply an insult and an attempt to
degrade Dublin. Is the next contemplated step to be
the ‘wiping out of the Dublin Depot altogether? We
hear a lot of the alleged running of Treland ab a loss;
but even if there is any truth in that, we must remem-
ber that Bngland takes care to have Treland to a large

Last week we referred to a complaint made by o
corrospondent to a local paper in connection with a
lecture in Waterford. This correspondent to the local
paper does not appear to have erred on the gide of
accuracy, if we may put it that way. We have heard
another side of the story. Tickets at half rates were
sold to nine Technical School students and to six
other students, and every Technical School student
who made application received a ticket at reduced rates.
There was no ** threepenny gallery,’” and the lads could
not have been packed like herrings there as the gal-
lery was not half full. We have before us a ticket,
upon which we read: ‘* Gallery and Body of Hall, 1s.”
These lectures, by the way, are managed by the Water-
ford Public Lectures’ Committee, and not by the Tech-
nical Committee. The first series were started in 1909
under the auspices of the Gilchrist Trust. Under such
auspices the lecturers’s fees and travelling expenses
are paid by the Trust; 25 per cent. of the seats were
reserved ab 1s. each, and all other seats were Id SN
the following session the Committee arranged for
another series of lectures, but for the second series they
had to pay lecturers’ fees amountling to £58 16s. 0d.,
and the total cost was £95 17s. 6d.; there were six lec-
tures and an average attendance of 327; that attend-
ance at 1s. a head all round would only yield £98 2s.—
just cover expenses. But the Committee, as a matter
of fact, provided accommodation for 100 at 6d., and
100 at 8d., and the loss on the year’s working was
£12 8s. 5d, For the present session arrangements
were made for a series of four lectures, three of which
have been held, and the average attendance for the
three has been only 186. There are 100 seats at 6d.
and 100 at 3d., and, assuming that the attendance at
the fourth will be up to the average of the previous
three, there will be quite a serious deficiency. We
understand that there will be no lectures next session.
Those who took part in the organisation and arrange-
ment of these lectures were all voluntary workers.

COOLBANAGHER AGAIN.

OUR readers must have thought that the Cool-
banagher rector had got enough and to spare
from Mr. J. P. 0’Kane. Having had time to regain
his composure after the dressing down that he received
from Ballycastle, Fe would now like to add to his noto-
riety by getting a dressing down from ‘‘ M. O'R.”
Let Brother Campbell, K.C., come along, and we have
no doubt that “M. O’R.” will engage him. The
windy production from Coolbanagher is not worthy of
the' considered notice of ‘‘ M. O’R.”’: indeed it has no
claim in reason on our space. However, let the Cool-
banagher rector have his say :—

THE MOTU PROPRIO.

“ M. O’R.”” has published in the Lavur of January
27 a clever defence of the recent ‘* Motu Proprio.”
His arguments will probably appear eminently satis-
factory to Roman Catholics. But he has failed to
grasp the real grounds of the objection to it on the part
of Protestants.

There is a good deal in the Protestant objections
which should gain a sympathetic hearing from thorough-
going Nationalists, Sinn Keiners, and Home Rulers.
Although Protestants are satisfied to be governed from
Westminster, because, on the whole, they believe that
they get fair play there, yet they object to be governed
from Rome, whose legislation they profoundly distrust.
To speak plainly, their chief objection to Home Rule
is that they fear it would mean Rome Rule. They are
not particularly enamoured with Westminster rule as
exhibited by the present regime; but they have no de-
sire to see Rome substituted for Westminster. This
would be, in their opinion, out of the frymg-pan into
the fire.

No one will deny that if a Home Rule Parliament
were set up in Dublin its members would be over-
whelmingly Roman Catholic. =~ Now, the allegiance
which the Church of Rome demands from her members
differs not only in degree but in kind from the allegiance
which all Protestant churches claim from their adhe-
rents. Take the Church of Ireland, to which I have
the honour to belong. It is essentially a National and
Home Rule Church. It is what the Greeks call an
autocephalous or self-governing church. While recog-
nising the existence and brotherhood of other
churches, and expressing its belief in the ‘‘ Holy
Catholic Church,” or the sum total of all who
““ profess and call themselves Christians,” it re-
cognises no binding authority outside the shores
of Ireland. Its supreme authority on earth
resides in its General Synod, consisting of the Bench
of Bishops, and a number of representatives of the
clergy and laity from the different dioceses, the laity
being in the proportion of two to one of the clergy. Its
principle is free and public discussion, and the right of
every member to express his opinion. There is no in-
stitution in Ireland which exhibits more perfectly the
democratic principle of free speech and individual
liberty than the General Synod of the Church of Ire-
land. Tts discussions on all subjects are published in
the public Press for the world to read.

Compare this with the Church of Rome. Its consti-
fution is not democratic but autocratic and monarchi-
cal. Its Head makes the tremendous claim of infalli-
bility in all matters concerning faith and morals. He
alone can determine the limits of his authority. He
claims absolute and unquestioning obedience from all
his subjects. He claims algo to be a temporal prince,
higher than the kings of the earth, and to set up and
put down all Christian princes as their liege lord. No
one but himself dare to attempt to limit the extent of
his claims. Although some of his claims, such as the
right to depose sovereigns from their thrones, and to

release their subjects from their allegiance, are nof
exercised at present, they have never been withdrawn,
and are only in abeyance. =

His privy council consists of some seventy Cardinals,
mostly Italians, divided into eleven congregations.
These issue from time to time their decrees upon all
kinds of subjects; and when the Pope signs his ngme
to them they must be received by all “ good Catholics
everywhere with unquestioning submission. 4

The laity have no part in the government of their
church. Whatever their private qpinions may be, they
are not allowed to express them in public. ;

This is the system against which Protestantism pro-
tests with all the energy it possesses. It is clean con-
trary to its own principles of the right of private judg-
ment for all, freedom of thought and freedom of ex-
pression.

Ireland has recently had two examples of this Rome
Rule in the Decree ‘“ Ne Temere,” and the ‘‘ Motu
Proprio,” “ Quantavis Diligentia.”” The former affects
the most intimate and sacred ties of human relation-
ship. According to this Decree a Roman Catholic can
only contract marriage with the consent of, practically,
one man. 1If that man refuses his consent, and he con-
tracts elsewhere, his marriage is declared to be null
and void, and he is deprived of the sacraments of his
church. Indescribable hardships have already been in-
flicted by this Decree in various parts of the world since
it was promulgated, and came into force on Easter Day,
1908. The McCann case has stirred Ireland to its
depths. Mrs. McCann still sits in her lonely lodgings,
husbandless, childless, and homeless, under this cruel
edict. And yet we look in vain for any protest from
Roman Catholics. Such are not the men to govern
the freedom-loving Protestants of Ireland.

Close upon the heels of ‘“ Ne Temere” follows the
*“ Motu Proprio.”” T confess that I see no force in the
arguments of those who try to prove that it does not
apply to Ireland. Surely the Pope holds that it ought
to apply to Ireland. If it does not, it can only be be-
cause Ireland is governed by British law, which is no
respecter of persons, not by Roman canon law. An
independent Parliament in Ireland, whose members
would be preponderatingly Roman Catholic, could not,
consistently with their professed principles, oppose it.
The Roman Church has struggled for centuries to secure
the immunity of the clexgy from the jurisdiction of lay
tribunals, and with two objects in view, 1, to enhance
the power and dignity of the clergy, and 2, to prevent
the publication of ‘‘ scandals’’ before the eyes of the
world. It is not merely, as some have contended, to
prevent unnecessary litigation among Roman Catholics.
If this were the case, it should apply to lawsuits among
the laity themselves, as well as to those between the
laity and the clergy. But what clinches the matter is
the word ‘* sacrilege,”” which the Pope uses to describe
the wickedness of the action- of anyone who dares to
cite an ‘‘ ecclesiastical person’’ before a lay tribunal.
Sacrilege is one of the most grievous sins in the eyes
of the Roman Church, and if it be sacrilege to cite an
ecclesiastic before a civil court in Italy, it must be
sacrilege to do so in Ireland. If an act is inherently
wrong, it must be wrong everywhere and always. A
wrong may be tolerated where there is no power to pre-
venb it. Bubt where circumstances are favourable, it
must be put down with a strong hand.

be said that everything depends upon the result

gated the law there. The writer asked

the British Islgs, and received the assurance that
only after the case has been examined by the

Holy See. The Governments of Belgium and of
Prussia, through their Ministers to the Holy See,

bably, a similar pronouncement, will be made rela-

“M. O'R.” quotes as a justification of the * Privi-
legium Fori’’ the following extract from the consti-
tutions of Trinity College:—'‘All domestic differences
shall be examined and, if possible, decided within the
college. He who brings another into court without the
consent of the Provost and the majority of the Senior
Fellows shall be expelled from the College.”” But this
statute applies equally all round to all members of Tri-
nity College, not to a particular class. To afford a
parallel to the ‘‘ Motu Proprio,” it should read—"* He
who brings a Fellow into court,” ete. Besides, many

« of these statutes are now out of date, and are being

revised.

When I speak of the Protestant principle of freedom
of speech, I shall probably be reminded of the action of
the Belfast Unionists with regard to Mr. Winston
Churchill’s intended speech there. I admit that their
action is inconsistent with their principles. It was dic-
tated more by strong feeling than by reason. We are
all inconsistent at times. If a man gives me a
slap in the face with a wet cat my duty would be to
call a policeman, and let a civil tribunal punish him.
But I would not guarantee that I should not, under
the influence of strong feeling, in such a case incon-
sistently take the law into my own hands. Many strong
Protestants are Home Rulers under the conviction that
under Home Rule Roman Catholics would be incon-
sistent with their principles, and that, so far from sub-
mitting to be governed from Rome, Dublin would be-
come more anti-Roman than Westminster, and would
take a leaf or two out of the statute-books of such
Roman Catholic countries as Italy, France, Spain and
Portugal. It may be so. I am only pointing out what,
as a matter of fact, the principles of Romanism are.
It is rather risky to act upon the assumption that men
will be inconsistent with their professed principles.

the clergy, as set forth in the ‘‘ Motu Proprio,”’ as a

Tor ourselves, as we are not theologians, it is
none of our business to enter into arguments involving
church matters, but as men of affairs we may make a
few comments on Coolbanagher’s production. It does
not require a subtle intellect to detect the shallow trick
of attempting to put up an antagonism between nation-
ality and the universal church. It is rather a common
trick with the Coolbanagher type. Some would put
country before religion, which is absurd, for if country
were before religion then one’s patriotism would be his
religion, and would be the final criterion of morals.
If it served ome’s country to kill, killing could be no

mate criterion. There are some shallow Nationalists

clear-headed Catholic boy, not to talk of man, can see
that there is no contradiction between Nationality and
the Catholic Church.

Coolbanagher objects ‘‘ to be governed from Rome’"!

rabble of Belfast, who fired stones at the windows of

not impose on our readers. It is a matter of indiffer-

and they don’t say by your leave to €oolbanagher nor

week. This talk of Home Rule being Rome Rule is a
phrase that has become a sort of idol before which the
Coolbmmghers tremble, or pretend to tremble. Take
its literal meaning for a moment. Is Rome to have the
control of the much talked of Customs and Tixcise ?

THE DALKEY RATEPAYERS’ ASSOCIATION.

lN a previous article I pointed out that on paper the

constitution and policy of the Dalkey Ratepayers’
Association is strictly non-sectarian and non-political,
but that in practice and in fact it is Conservative, Pro-
testant and Masonic. In the present article I wish
further to point out that the Association 1s not repre-
sentative of the great bulk of the ratepayers of Dalkey,
that its policy and the work of its representatives on
the Urban Council for 1911 have been condemned by
the electors, and that its candidates at the'last elec-
tions who pledged themselves to the hilt in support of
the policy of the Association met with complete dis-
aster at the poll.

1. The Dalkey Ratepayers’ Association is a self-con-
stituted body. It is not elected by the entire body or,
even by a majority of the ratepayers of Dalkey, and
therefore cannot represent them. It represents nobody
except Conservatives, Protestants and Masons. It
cannot represent Nationalists and Catholics because
they don’t want to be represented by any people except:
themselves, and their everlasting cry is ‘‘ hands off,
Conservatives and Masons.”” Moreover, the members
of the Ratepayers’ Association don’t want to represent
Nationalists and Catholics. If they did they would
seek their co-operation as a body, which they have not
done. They simply want to get a sufficient number
of Nationalists and Catholics into the Association in
order to split the Nationalist vote. If they desired to
co-operate why did their ropresentatives on the Urban

Council elect a Conservative and Protestant chairman,

of the township.” J

. 2. The result of the year’s work of the representa-
tives of the  Association on the Urban Council has been
condemned by a majority of the electors after a defi-
nite and distinct appeal was made to them for a favour-
able verdict. g

** The result of the year’s work of the Council,”” wrote
sleek Mr. Booth ‘and his Masonic friends, ‘* is known
to the electors, and their verdict in favour of the con-
tinuation of a policy which has proved so successful is
looked forward to with every confidence.’’

The verdict must have brought shocking disappoint-
ment. It was complete, emphatic and remarkable.
The four Ratepayers’ Association candidates were re-
jected at the poll, one of whom, Mr. Leckey, must have
sat on the Council for eight or nine years. Thé majo-
rity of the electors replied we don’t want a continuation
of your policy. There is too much Masonry about it
and too much Toryism.

Even the work of the Council in connection with
the purchase of Dalkey Island was specifically men-
tioned by Mr. Booth and his friends in order to catch
the fishermen’s votes. The fishermen were told in the
election circular that ‘‘ owing to the initiative of the
representatives of the Association on the Council, the
negotiations with regard to the purchase of Dalkey Is-
land are at such an advanced stage that a satisfactory
issue may be expected at an early date. I'ree access
to the Island has been a source of income to
the fishermen and hoat owners, who form one of the
oldest portions of the community.’’

One would imagine from this patronising paragraph
that *‘ free access to the Island "’ and the benefit to the
fishermen were obtained, conferred and bequeafhed as
a precious legacy to the people of Dalkey by the Tories
since the establishment of their Ratepayers’ Associa-
tion. The fact is that the Island is the property of the
War Office, and every Tom, Dick and Polly have had
*“ free access '’ to it for generations.

In 1910 when the Nationalists had a majority on the
Council they had almost completed negotiations to
obtain it gratis from the War Office and convert it into
a public park. We now hear that the Conservatiyes
on the Council are about to purchase it from the War
Office for a considerable sum of money, which means
of course an additional tax on the unfortunate rate-
payers of Dalkey. .

As regards the benefit to the fishermen, the Tories
and the members of their Ratepayers’ Association
ought to be ashamed of themselves for the cold-shoulder
policy they have adopted towards these ‘‘ oldest por-
tions of the community.”

What did they do during 1911 to provide a shelter
for these honest, deserving, hard-working men? Did
they not know that throughout the entive year these
men spend several hours every day at Coliemore Har-
bour in connection with their occupation as fishermen?
Did they not know that these ‘‘oldest portions of the
community ’’ are frequently there for, hours before
going to fish, mending and arranging their fishing lines,
preparing bait and putting it on hook{s?—plonsnnb work
enough in fine weather, but in rain and snow and
frost, and especially when the piercing North and
East winds blow, is not at all pleasant.  Did they
not know that the work done by the ‘‘ oldest portions

When the deliberations of the Unionist Party meet-

ing, held before the re-assembling of Parliament, had ,

(134 HANEL,”’ in his ““ The Sect of the Gael,” has
voiced the misgivings of the Gaelic Leaguer
grown a little old and a little weary. He has done so
at an opportune time, I think. TFor one reason or
another Irish Ireland is at present rather off the stage,
and it cannot afford that. Irish itself can live only
by being spoken, and Irish Ireland, as things are, can
live only by being spoken about.

The object of the -Gaelic League is to preserve the
historic Irish nation by making Irish the ordinary
medium of communication between Iishmen. It
has not wrought this latter miracle as yet, *“ Chanel
says, and he underlines the ‘‘yet.” Underlining,
however, hardly insists enough on the importance of
the word. For it is all too easy for all of us to notice
how slow our progress is, and we cannot too con-
stantly reflect that the fight we are engaged in is one
that will lasb, not for years, but for generations. It
is all right to spur people on by telling them that, if
the Irish language is mot saved in this generation, it
won’t be saved at all, but that is, at best, rather a

. loose way of phrasing things.

When a man is sick you can’t very well tell at what
precise moment he must jump out of bed if he’s to
recover, and you can’t lay down any precise limit for
the Irish language either. The language may be safe
at the present moment, for all we know—I believe
myself it is safe—but that doesn’t mean that it could
by any possibility live if left to itself now. No, we
have got to nurse it, and to keep on nursing it, and,
with all the nursing we can give it, it will take genera-
tions before it is the ‘‘ ordinary medium of communi-
cation between Irishmen.”’ The thing is not to
despair. To keep pegging away. Languages aren’t
things that can be learnt by an earnest student, much
less saved to a more or less careless nation, in a year
or two.

MAVOURNEEN.

Her eyes are like the fabled gems

That sparkle on some jewelled crown,
But never wealth of diadems

Could match the glory of her own.
Her thoughts are prayers, and chaster far

Than snow-flakes drifting down the sky,
Her soul is like a fixéd star

Calm and serene in constancy.

Through lonely hours, through weary days,
The world between—yet hand in hand—
Her spirit guides my devious ways,
We know, we feel, we understand.
Sweet love perpetual wraps us round,
Flows forth in streams of fadeless fire—
Queen of my fate, whom Love has crowned,
Star of my longing soul’s desire!

HALF-YBARLY CONTRACTS.

The Board of Guardians of this Union will, at their
Moating to bo held on Wednesday, the 21st day of
Fobruary, 1912, recoive, and consider Tendors for
the supply and delivery at tho Workhouse for six
months, from the First of April Noxt, of Best Woll-
Fed Irvish Bred Meat, as described in Tonder Forms,
viz. :—Haunches of Beef, to weigh not less than 75lbs.,
not including lap or log; Stiocking Pieces; Cloan
Carcases of Bost Wother Irish Mutton, to woigh not
less than 90lbs., nor more than 115lbs.; TLogs of
Mutton, not including lap ; Best Seconds Irish Flour ;
Best Wheatmeal and Cut Oatmeal (Irish); Indian
Meal, all of which must bo equal to samples to bo
seen in stores, and must be ground in Irish mills.
A certificate to that offect must accompany each
de_llilvery« signed by the Contractor and owners of the
mill.

The Bread Contract for daily delivery at the
Auxiliary, Cabra, to be as follows:—Fresh 3lb.
Loaves, at per lb., the approximate quantity
being about 120 loaves per day; the loaves to
be made from the best seconds flour of same stan-
dard quality as above advertised, and the samo
conditions to be observed as to grinding, ete.,
a sample loaf to be submitted with the Tender.
Also best Irich Eggs, 120 to weigh I5lbs.; Irish
Bacon (Middles), to be branded by the maker,
persons tendering to state the brand, weight to
range from 20 to 26lbs.. or Three-quarter Bacon,
to weigh from 28 to 30lb. per side, boned and
shoulder blades out, except rib (Irish); Irish
Chickens to weigh not less than 1}lb. each; Best
Sugar, as per standard sample, to be seen in
Master’s Stores; Fish—Whiting, Plaice, Ling, ete.;
Best Irish Table Potatoes ; Best Home-grown Fodder
—namely, First Crop Hay, Oaten and Wheaten
Straw, none to be baled ; Best Black Oats, 38lbs.
to the bushel ; Best White Bran.

Also Tenders are invited for Best Creamery
Butter for six months from lst April, 1912, in
accordance with terms of Guardians’ resolution, as
set forth on special Tender Forms.

Attention is directed to the Guardians’ resolution
as to fair houses and the employment of Trade
Union labour, the standard rate of wages, and to
their resolution against sub-letting any part of their
contract, and the power to visit any factory or
factories, during working hours, where any of their
contracts are in progress.

All Forms of Tender can be had at the Master’s
Stores, and the lowest or any Tender not necessarily
accepted. The Guardians reserve to themselves
the right to sub-divide any of the above contracts.
Attention is also directed to Section 51 (7) of the

“  Local Government Act, 1898, which restricts pay-
ment of all accounts to within three months after
the termination of each half-year.  Accouints to be
furnished monthly. Goods of Irish manufacture must
bear the Irish Trade Mark. Tenders, which must
be sealed (with wax), must not be put in envelopes;
but must be folded down with the printed endorse-
ment on back. Sealed Tenders to bo placed in the
Tender Box here up to 12 o'clock noon on Wednes-
day, 21st day of February, 1912, upon which or the
following days they will be considered.

Security will be required for the due performance
of each contract.
